sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Adult cardiac arrest is a time-critical emergency. Management should follow current ANZCOR adult advanced life support principles, with immediate recognition, high-quality chest compressions, timely defibrillation when indicated, minimisation of interruptions, and active consideration of reversible causes.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Unresponsive and not breathing normally
- No signs of life
- Agonal respirations may be present and should not be mistaken for effective breathing
- If in doubt, begin resuscitation while the rhythm and situation are clarified unless obvious death or injuries incompatible with life are present

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety comes first
- High-quality CPR and defibrillation remain the core priorities
- Advanced interventions must not create unnecessary interruptions in compressions
- Pulse checks are only appropriate when an organised rhythm is present or when there are signs of ROSC
- Team leadership, role allocation, and clear communication improve resuscitation quality
- Follow the current ANZCOR adult ALS algorithm for compression rate, shock sequencing, medications, and reversible-cause management

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Confirm cardiac arrest and call for appropriate help
- Start chest compressions immediately and provide ventilations using the current recommended approach
- Attach a defibrillator or AED as early as possible
- Deliver shocks when indicated with minimal interruption to compressions
- Continue two-minute CPR cycles with rhythm assessment and reassessment according to ANZCOR principles

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Advanced airway, vascular access, and medications should only be introduced when they do not significantly interrupt high-quality CPR
- Use adrenaline and amiodarone according to the current ANZCOR adult ALS algorithm
- Consider a supraglottic airway first where appropriate and within scope
- Actively search for and treat reversible causes throughout the arrest
- Transport during active arrest should only occur when there is a clear clinical or operational reason and it will not significantly worsen CPR quality

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Refer to the current ANZCOR adult advanced life support algorithm for exact sequencing of shocks, medications, and post-ROSC transition
- Excellent compressions, timely defibrillation, and treatment of reversible causes have the greatest effect on outcome
